text: So what we're looking at is we're doing a robotic prostatectomy, and there's different ways to do it.
The surgeon performs a robotic prostatectomy using a transperitoneal approach, focusing on the posterior aspect of the prostate.
The camera view shows the interior of the bowels, with visible intestinal loops and surrounding peritoneal structures.
The surgical team navigates within the peritoneal cavity during a robotic prostatectomy, utilizing a transperitoneal approach.
The surgeon uses the right hand to point at the bladder, visible within the surgical field during the robotic prostatectomy.
The surgeon identifies a bulge, which is the balloon of the Foley catheter, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon pushes up on the bladder to initiate the posterior approach transperitoneally, positioning the bladder for further dissection.
The surgeon cuts the peritoneum located underneath the bladder, using a surgical instrument to carefully incise the tissue.
The assistant uses a sucker port to provide upwards traction while the surgeon applies downwards traction to mobilize the seminal vesicles and vasa.
And with the posterior approach, that's what we do first.
The surgeon has freed the tissues on the right side during the posterior approach, revealing the anatomical structures beneath.
The surgeon holds the white tubular structure of the vas deferens with the left hand, preparing to cut it.
The surgeon cuts the vasa deferens, releasing yellowish fluid identified as semen.
The surgeon proceeds to free the left seminal vesicle, having already freed the right one, using careful dissection techniques to separate it from surrounding tissues.
The seminal vesicle contains a lot of nutrients and fluid that help the sperm live.
The surgeon holds the freed seminal vesicles with the left hand, although they are partially out of view in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses scissors to make an incision into the non-VA's fascia, a layer of connective tissue surrounding the prostate.
The surgeon carefully moves the rectum away from the prostate, ensuring clear separation between these anatomical structures.
The surgeon uses their right hand to gently push the rectum away, while the prostate is held up above, creating a clear view of the surgical field.
The surgeon uses electrocautery with the left hand to cauterize the superficial dorsal vein, then cuts and removes the vein from the surgical field.
The surgeon removes excess fat from above the prostate to improve visibility, revealing the walnut-shaped prostate beneath.
The shiny surface visible on the left and right sides of the prostate is identified as the endopelvic fascia.
The surgeon uses scissors to create a plane between the bladder and the prostate after cutting the endopelvic fascia, carefully separating these structures.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the tissue to maintain a dry surgical field, allowing for the visualization of bladder fibers forming a funnel shape, which aids in creating the bladder neck.
The surgeon opens the bladder neck, revealing the yellow Foley catheter as it becomes visible within the surgical field.

The surgeon uses the fourth robotic arm to latch onto the eyelet and apply traction, while the assistant pulls on the Foley catheter externally. This maneuver elevates the prostate, facilitating dissection on the posterior wall of the bladder.
The surgeon attempts to create a small bladder neck, which is visible as a funnel-shaped structure, to facilitate easier anastomosis during the prostatectomy.
The surgeon identifies the bladder neck as a pink circular area located centrally below the yellow Foley catheter, ensuring it is adequately sized to avoid accidental entry into the prostate.
The surgeon peels and dissects the bladder away from the prostate, having already separated the anterior bladder, and now proceeds to detach the posterior bladder from the prostate.
The seminal vesicles and vasa deferentia, previously dissected, come into view as the surgeon completes the connection between anatomical structures during the prostatectomy.
The surgeon uses the fourth robotic arm to pull up the seminal vesicles, with the right vesicle visible in the center of the surgical field, while preparing to elevate the left vesicle.
The surgeon begins dissecting the pedicles and neurovascular bundles on the left side, carefully separating these structures from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon places a permanent clip on the pedicle, which serves as the blood supply to the prostate, ensuring it remains secure without causing harm to the patient.

The surgeon places an additional clip on the pedicle to minimize back bleeding, preparing to continue the dissection through the rest of the pedicle. Multiple clips are used, potentially increasing in number based on the prostate size.
After controlling the pedicle, the surgeon begins dissecting the neurovascular bundle, gently pulling away surrounding tissue to preserve nerve structures.
The surgeon gently dissects the vascular tissue surrounding the neurovascular bundles, carefully peeling it away to minimize trauma and avoid tearing into the prostate.
The surgeon completes the dissection on the left side, revealing the shiny white surface of the prostate. The procedure will now proceed to the right side of the prostate.
The surgeon isolates the prostate, focusing on the dorsal vein complex. The dorsal vein is tied off, with minimal bleeding observed, allowing further freeing of the neurovascular bundle.
The surgeon ties off the dorsal vein complex using a slip knot after completing the dissection of the neurovascular bundles.

The surgeon places an additional suture around the dorsal vein complex, incorporating part of the urethra, and brings it up to the pubic symphysis. The suture is rolled one or two times to cinch the complex, aiming to reconstruct the angle lost after cutting through the puboprostatic ligaments.

The surgeon performs anterior reconstruction and ties the dorsal vein complex, cutting it to expose the urethra. The dissection is kept close to the knot, ensuring the fibers are not violating the prostate. The neurovascular bundles are visible laterally, allowing safe use of cautery with cooling irrigation fluid to prevent thermal spread.

The surgeon applies extra cautery to the area around the prostate, ensuring that the neurovascular bundles are safely away from the cauterized region. This step aims to eliminate any minute prostate cancer cells present.

The urethra, appearing as a tubular structure, is visible as the surgeon dissects the top half before cutting it open to expose the Foley catheter. The prostate tissue is pulled away, revealing the Foley catheter within the urethra, which is a long tube that aids in patient continence.

The surgeon frees the posterior part of the urethra, with minimal active bleeding visible in the surgical field. The dissection continues through the recto-urethralis tissue, and the prostate is subsequently freed.

The surgeon places the excised prostate into a containment bag to prevent the spread of prostate cancer cells. The anastomosis begins using a quill suture, preparing to connect the urethra to the bladder.

The surgeon uses sutures as ice picks to grasp the posterior bladder, exposing the mucosa. The assistant gently pulls down on the anterior bladder neck, allowing the mucosa to be visible. The surgeon then pulls one needle through and subsequently brings both needles out simultaneously.

The assistant pushes the Foley catheter into the bladder, while the surgeon follows the catheter and twists to secure the posterior urethra with a suture. The surgeon uses their left hand to grasp both ends of the suture, applying traction to the urethra. The assistant repositions the catheter, and the surgeon repeats the steps, placing a six o'clock suture on both the bladder and urethra.
The surgeon places an additional suture on the bladder side before pulling the bladder and urethra together, preparing for anastomosis without posterior reconstruction.
The surgeon gently tugs on the bladder and urethra until they are in contact with each other, ensuring good apposition. The use of a quill suture helps prevent slippage during this process.
The surgeon employs a Van Veltoven suture technique, starting at the six o'clock position and progressing upwards to the twelve o'clock position, securing the bladder and urethra together.

The surgeon completes suturing on one side, bringing the suture through the urethra near the twelve o'clock position. The suture is released without using the fourth arm for tension, ensuring no gaps on the left side. The surgeon then prepares to repeat the suturing steps on the right side.

The surgeon completes the anastomosis at the twelve o'clock position, breaking the suture with the robotic instrument. One or two suture throws are made to secure the anastomosis, followed by testing for leakage by filling the bladder with fluid. A drain is placed through a port site to monitor for bleeding or fluid leakage. The abdominal pressure is reduced to check for bleeding before removing the robotic instruments and closing the ports.
